ingredients_notes: Primary components include poached or steamed bone-in or boneless chicken (150-180g), fragrant chicken-fat rice cooked in broth (200-220g), chicken broth (100-120ml), cucumber slices (30-40g), chilli sauce (15-20g), and condiments including dark soy sauce and ginger paste (10-15g). Chicken is poached in a master stock with ginger, garlic, and pandan leaves then ice-bathed for silky skin. Rice is toasted in rendered chicken fat and cooked in poaching broth with garlic and ginger.
dietary_considerations: High sodium content: 900-1200mg per plate represents 39-52% of the recommended daily limit of 2300mg
Contains gluten from dark soy sauce and light soy sauce
May contain sesame oil depending on recipe
Contains garlic and onion in rice and broth preparation
Not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to chicken and chicken-based broth
Not suitable for low-carb or ketogenic diets due to high carbohydrate content from rice
Diabetic individuals should exercise caution due to high glycaemic index rice
Suitable for Halal diets when sourced from certified Halal stalls
Naturally dairy-free
Can be modified for gluten-free diets by requesting tamari or no soy sauce
Removing chicken skin reduces fat by approximately 4-6g and calories by 40-55 kcal
Excellent source of complete protein with all essential amino acids
Collagen-rich gelatin present in broth from poaching method
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Singapore hawker centre preparation. Values are approximate ranges and may vary depending on the specific stall, chicken size, rice portion, and condiment usage. Data referenced from Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) nutritional databases, USDA FoodData Central, and established food science literature.

iron_mg.note: From cockles, which are exceptionally iron-rich

ingredients_notes: Flat rice noodles (kway teow), Chinese lap cheong (pork sausage), cockles, prawns, bean sprouts, eggs, chives, dark soy sauce, light soy sauce, and lard or cooking oil. Traditional hawker-style preparation uses lard, contributing to higher saturated fat and caloric content.
dietary_considerations: Contains shellfish (prawns and cockles) — major allergen
Contains gluten — soy sauces typically contain wheat
Contains eggs
Contains pork — lap cheong (Chinese sausage) and lard used in authentic preparation
High sodium — 1,400 to 1,800mg per plate represents 60 to 78 percent of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2,300mg
High caloric density — combination of refined carbohydrates, lard or oil, and sausage
High cholesterol — contributed by cockles, eggs, and lard
Refined carbohydrates — flat rice noodles have a high glycaemic index
Not suitable for Halal diets — contains pork and lard
Not suitable for vegetarian or vegan diets
Not suitable for low-sodium diets
Not suitable for low-fat diets
Not typically gluten-free due to soy sauce containing wheat
Good source of iron from cockles
Good source of Vitamin B12 from shellfish and egg
Moderate protein contribution from prawns, cockles, egg, and sausage
Versions prepared with vegetable oil instead of lard are slightly lower in saturated fat and calories, approximately 580 to 640 kcal
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Singapore hawker-style preparation, cross-referenced with the Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) nutritional data, USDA FoodData Central, and established food science literature. Actual values may vary by hawker stall and preparation style.

iron_mg.notes: From cockles, prawns and fish cake

vitamin_c_mg.notes: From chilli, lemongrass and laksa leaves

ingredients_notes: Thick rice vermicelli or laksa noodles in a coconut milk-based broth built on a rempah spice paste of lemongrass, galangal, dried shrimp, candlenuts, and chilli. Typically garnished with prawns, fish cake, tofu puffs, cockles, and fresh laksa leaves (daun kesum). Shrimp paste (belacan) contributes significantly to sodium content.
dietary_considerations: Very high saturated fat — coconut milk base may exceed 100% of recommended daily saturated fat intake for some individuals
High sodium — up to 1,500 mg per serving, representing approximately 60–65% of WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg
Moderate cholesterol content from prawns and cockles
Contains shellfish allergen — prawns and cockles present
Contains fish allergen — fish cake and dried shrimp present
Contains soy allergen — tofu puffs present
Possible gluten presence — some fish cakes and soy-based ingredients may contain gluten
Possible tree nut presence — candlenuts used in rempah paste
Eggs occasionally present — half a hard-boiled egg sometimes added as garnish
Not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to seafood and shrimp paste
Not suitable for low-sodium or low-fat diets
Generally Halal — verify cockles and preparation method
Dairy-free
Good high-protein option — 28 to 32g protein per serving
To reduce calorie and sodium intake by 15–25%, consider requesting less broth and avoiding finishing the soup
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Singapore preparation methods. Values derived from the Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Food Composition Database and USDA FoodData Central. Nutritional values may vary depending on the hawker stall, restaurant, or home recipe used.

portion_size: 1 plate (approximately 500-600g including shell, ~250g edible crab meat with sauce)
ingredients_notes: Primary protein source is whole mud crab (Scylla serrata) or Sri Lankan crab. Sauce base includes fresh red chillies, chilli paste, sambal, tomato ketchup, and tomato paste. Beaten eggs are stirred into the sauce to create silky ribbons. Aromatics include garlic, ginger, shallots, and lemongrass. Seasoned with fermented shrimp paste (belacan), soy sauce, oyster sauce, and palm sugar or regular sugar. Sauce is thickened with a cornstarch slurry and cooked in vegetable or palm oil. Typically served with mantou buns (not included in nutritional values; add approximately 150-200 kcal per 2 buns).
dietary_considerations: Contains shellfish (crustacean) - major allergen; not suitable for individuals with shellfish allergies
Contains eggs - present in the sauce
Contains soy - derived from soy sauce and oyster sauce
Contains gluten - oyster sauce and standard soy sauces contain wheat; not gluten-free in standard preparation
Contains shrimp/prawn paste (belacan) - additional shellfish allergen

High sodium content: 1400-1800mg per serving represents approximately 60-78% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2300mg; caution advised for individuals managing hypertension, kidney disease, or cardiovascular conditions
Elevated dietary cholesterol from crab; individuals monitoring cholesterol intake should be mindful of portion size
Suitable for pescatarians
Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians
Not suitable for gluten-free diets under standard preparation
Dairy-free
Moderate suitability for keto or low-carb diets due to added sugars and carbohydrates in the sauce
Partial suitability for paleo diets; processed sauces such as ketchup and oyster sauce are generally excluded under strict paleo guidelines
Halal-suitable; crab is generally accepted under most Halal standards
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Singapore preparation methods. Nutritional values may vary depending on restaurant, chef, crab size, and sauce quantity. All values are calculated excluding shell weight. Mantou buns served as an accompaniment are not included in the stated nutritional figures.

ingredients_notes: Core ingredients include pork ribs, garlic, white pepper, dark and light soy sauce, and optionally dried mushrooms and tofu puffs. Served with steamed white rice and you tiao (fried dough fritters). Singapore-style broth is clear and peppery, distinct from the darker herb-heavy Malaysian Klang style. Long bone-simmering leaches calcium and collagen into the broth.
dietary_considerations: Very high sodium — soy sauce and seasoning may contribute 75–80% of the recommended daily sodium limit (2,000–2,300 mg); caution advised for those with hypertension or kidney concerns
Moderate to high saturated fat — particularly if spare ribs are used rather than lean back ribs
Moderate to high cholesterol — relevant for those managing cardiovascular health
Contains gluten — soy sauce typically contains wheat; use tamari or gluten-free soy sauce for gluten-free preparation
Contains soy — present in soy sauce and tofu puffs if included
No shellfish — typically absent from this dish
No nuts
No dairy
Not suitable for vegetarian or vegan diets
Not suitable for halal diets in traditional preparation due to pork content
Not suitable for low-sodium diets without significant modification
Suitable for dairy-free diets
Suitable for high-protein dietary goals
Full meal with rice and you tiao can total 800–900+ kcal
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Singapore preparation methods, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central, Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) nutritional data, and established food composition tables. Actual values may vary by restaurant, recipe, and cut of pork used.

ingredients_notes: Yellow egg noodles and rice vermicelli stir-fried in prawn and pork broth, served with prawns, squid, pork belly slices, eggs, and bean sprouts. Accompanied by sambal chilli, calamansi lime, and crispy lard. Seasoned with soy sauce and cooked using wok hei technique.
dietary_considerations: Contains shellfish — prawns are a primary ingredient (major allergen)
Contains molluscs — squid is commonly included
Contains gluten — yellow egg noodles are made with wheat flour
Contains eggs — used in the stir-fry
Contains soy — soy sauce used in seasoning

Contains pork and lard — not suitable for halal diets
Not suitable for vegetarians or vegans
Not suitable for those with shellfish or mollusc allergies
Not suitable for coeliac disease or gluten intolerance unless modified

High in complete protein from multiple seafood and egg sources
Excellent source of Vitamin B12 and Selenium from prawns and squid

Actual values may vary depending on the hawker stall, oil quantity used, portion size, and whether lard or vegetable oil is used. Values derived from USDA FoodData Central and Singapore HPB Nutrient Composition Database.

ingredients_notes: Coconut milk rice cooked with pandan leaves, fried egg (1 whole), fried anchovies (ikan bilis), roasted peanuts, fresh cucumber slices, and sambal chili paste. Optional additions such as fried chicken wing, otah, or beef rendang are not included in this base nutritional estimate.
dietary_considerations: High sodium content from sambal, anchovies, and seasoned rice - individuals managing hypertension should be mindful
High saturated fat from coconut milk used in rice preparation
Moderate-to-high calorie density - can increase by 200-400 kcal with optional additions such as fried chicken, rendang, or otah
Contains seafood allergen - anchovies (ikan bilis)
Contains egg allergen - fried egg

May contain gluten - verify sambal preparation

Not recommended for low-sodium diets

Diabetic individuals should exercise moderate caution due to high refined carbohydrate content

Anchovies and peanuts provide valuable protein, iron, and healthy fats despite being calorie-dense
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Singaporean preparation methods and established food composition databases including USDA and HPB Singapore Food Composition. Actual values may vary depending on hawker stall preparation, portion size, and specific ingredients used.

ingredients_notes: Thinly sliced chicken thigh or beef marinated with lemongrass, turmeric, galangal, coriander powder, coconut milk, and palm sugar, then charcoal-grilled on bamboo skewers. Served with a peanut dipping sauce made from ground roasted peanuts, dried chillies, shallots, garlic, lemongrass, coconut milk, tamarind, palm sugar, and shrimp paste (belacan). Accompanied by ketupat (compressed rice cake wrapped in coconut leaves), fresh cucumber, and raw onion wedges.
dietary_considerations: Contains peanuts — major allergen
Contains shellfish — shrimp paste (belacan) used in peanut sauce
Potential gluten traces from marinades and sauces — not guaranteed gluten-free
Moderately high sodium — those managing hypertension should be mindful
Elevated saturated fat due to coconut milk in marinade and peanut sauce
Notable sugar content from palm sugar in marinade and peanut sauce
Typically Halal-certified at most Singapore stalls — pork-free
Not suitable for vegetarians or vegans — contains meat and shrimp paste
High protein content — suitable for active individuals
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Singapore hawker-style preparation using a mixed chicken and beef satay plate with peanut sauce, ketupat, and sliced cucumber and onion. Actual values may vary depending on the stall, portion size, and specific recipe used.

portion_size: 1 plate (2 plain prata pieces + curry dipping sauce)
ingredients_notes: Primary ingredients include all-purpose plain flour, ghee (clarified butter) used in dough and on griddle, water, salt, and a small amount of sugar. Egg is added in egg prata variants. Condensed milk is sometimes incorporated for softness. Served with a curry dipping sauce, typically fish curry or mutton curry, which contributes additional sodium and fat.
dietary_considerations: Contains gluten (wheat flour) — not suitable for coeliac disease or gluten intolerance
Contains dairy (ghee) — not suitable for dairy-free or vegan diets
Curry dipping sauce often contains fish — check for fish and shellfish allergies
High in saturated fat due to ghee — consume in moderation if managing cardiovascular health
Moderate to high sodium content, especially with curry — relevant for those managing hypertension
Generally Halal-certified at mamak stalls
Plain prata with vegetable curry is lacto-vegetarian friendly
Not vegan due to ghee and potential egg content
Energy-dense dish — low in dietary fiber and high in refined carbohydrates
Consider pairing with dhal curry for added fiber and plant-based protein
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Singaporean hawker-style preparation. Actual values may vary depending on the stall, portion size, amount of ghee used, and type of curry served.

iron_mg.source_note: From yellow noodles, egg and tofu

diet_suitability.halal: Yes — when prepared at Mamak or Muslim stalls
diet_suitability.vegetarian: Possible — request no prawns or meat
diet_suitability.vegan: Possible — request no egg and no oyster sauce
diet_suitability.diabetic_friendly: Caution — high refined carbohydrates and sugars

ingredients_notes: Yellow wheat noodles stir-fried with eggs, tomatoes, green chillies, bean sprouts, tofu, and a sauce blend of tomato ketchup, chilli sauce, sweet soy sauce (kecap manis), and curry powder. Commonly includes prawns, mutton, or chicken. Typically garnished with a squeeze of lime.

High refined carbohydrates — caution advised for those managing blood sugar
Contains gluten from yellow wheat noodles
Contains eggs
Commonly contains shellfish (prawns)

Calorie-dense and carbohydrate-heavy — best consumed as an occasional treat
Sauce contains added sugars from ketchup and kecap manis contributing to glycaemic load

vitamin_c_mg.notes: From chye sim and bok choy

ingredients_notes: Thin springy egg noodles tossed in a savoury-sweet char siu sauce, served with pork and prawn wontons, char siu (Chinese BBQ pork), and leafy greens (chye sim or bok choy). Finished with lard or vegetable oil, sesame oil, and a light soy-based sauce. Chilli sauce commonly served on the side.
dietary_considerations: High sodium content: approximately 1,450–1,600 mg per plate, representing 60–70% of the recommended daily intake of 2,000 mg
Not suitable for halal diets due to presence of pork (char siu) and lard
Not suitable for vegetarian or vegan diets due to pork, prawns, eggs, and lard
Not suitable for gluten-free diets due to egg noodles, soy sauce, and oyster sauce

Contains egg allergen: egg noodles

Dry-style version is slightly higher in fat compared to soup-style; soup-style may have higher overall sodium from broth

Individuals managing hypertension or kidney conditions should consume with caution due to high sodium levels
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Singapore hawker preparation methods for a standard 350–400g plate. Values may vary depending on the hawker stall, recipe, and portion size. Consult a registered dietitian for personalised dietary advice.

vitamin_b12_mcg.note: Very high — from oysters and eggs

vitamin_c_mg.note: Small amount — from chilli sauce and garnish

phosphorus_mg.note: Good source — from eggs and oysters

ingredients_notes: Key ingredients include fresh or brined small oysters (80–100g), 2–3 whole eggs, tapioca or potato starch slurry (40–50g dry equivalent), lard or vegetable oil (2–3 tbsp), fish sauce or light soy sauce (1–2 tsp), spring onions or coriander (small amount), and a tangy chilli dipping sauce (1–2 tbsp). The tapioca starch slurry creates the dish's signature crispy-edged, chewy texture.
dietary_considerations: Contains shellfish (oysters) — major allergen; unsuitable for those with shellfish allergies
Contains eggs in significant quantity — unsuitable for those with egg allergies
May contain gluten — fish sauce and soy sauce may contain gluten; not suitable for coeliac disease unless gluten-free sauces are confirmed
Traditionally cooked with lard — not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding pork products

High dietary cholesterol (380–430 mg per plate) — primarily from eggs and oysters; individuals with hypercholesterolaemia should consume in moderation
Excellent source of zinc and vitamin B12 from oysters
Rich in selenium — an important antioxidant mineral
Halal status: oysters are generally considered halal; however, lard use must be verified with the specific hawker stall
Not suitable for kosher diets due to shellfish content and mixing of ingredients
Not suitable for vegetarian or vegan diets
Pescatarians may consume if lard is substituted with vegetable oil

Values referenced against USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB Nutrient Composition Database, and food science literature.

ingredients_notes: Primary ingredients include steamed white radish (daikon) and rice flour cake, eggs (1-2 per plate), preserved radish (chai poh), garlic, spring onions, fish sauce or light soy sauce, and lard or vegetable oil. The black version additionally includes sweet dark soy sauce (kicap manis), which adds approximately 20-30 kcal and 3-5g of sugar. Despite the name, no carrots are used in this dish.
dietary_considerations: High sodium content: approximately 900-1100mg per plate, representing 40-48% of the recommended daily intake of 2300mg
Contains eggs - not suitable for vegans or those with egg allergies
May contain traces of shellfish if cooked on shared hawker griddles
Generally low in gluten due to rice flour base, but soy sauce may contain wheat - not suitable for those with coeliac disease without vendor confirmation
Traditional preparation uses lard - not suitable for Muslims, vegetarians, or vegans
Some modern stalls substitute vegetable oil for lard - always confirm with vendor
Not typically suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to eggs, fish sauce, and lard; some vegetarian-friendly versions exist at specific stalls
Moderate-to-high caloric density driven by oil or lard content and starchy radish cake base

data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Singapore hawker preparation methods. Values are most representative of the black version unless otherwise noted and may vary between individual stalls. Nutritional figures represent approximate ranges rather than exact values.

ingredients_notes: Steamed white rice served with richly spiced side dishes including beef or chicken rendang, sayur lodeh (coconut milk vegetable curry), sambal goreng (tempeh or tofu in chilli paste), and sambal chilli condiment. Key cooking ingredients include coconut milk, chilli pastes, shrimp paste (belacan), aromatic spices, kecap manis, and candlenuts. Originally a Minangkabau dish from West Sumatra, widely adopted as a Singapore hawker staple.
dietary_considerations: High sodium content: 1,100–1,400 mg per plate represents 48–61% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2,300 mg; caution advised for individuals with hypertension
High saturated fat due to extensive use of coconut milk; frequent consumption may pose cardiovascular health concerns
Calorie-dense meal combining rice with rich coconut-based curries
Contains shrimp paste (belacan) — not suitable for shellfish-allergic individuals or strict vegetarians and vegans
May contain soy allergens from tempeh, tofu, and kecap manis
May contain gluten from soy sauce or wheat-based ingredients in certain sides
May contain tree nuts (candlenuts) in spice pastes
Typically halal-certified at Muslim-run hawker stalls
To reduce calories, opt for less rice and choose vegetable-based sides over rendang
To reduce sodium, limit sambal and gravy ladled over rice
Brown rice substitution recommended for added fiber and lower glycemic impact where available
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Singapore hawker-style preparation. Values represent mid-range estimates for a standard plate with rice and three common sides: rendang, sayur lodeh, and sambal goreng. Actual nutritional content may vary significantly depending on stall, cook, and specific side dish selection. Individual components should be assessed separately for precise dietary tracking.

calcium_mg.note: From fish bones and coconut milk

iron_mg.note: From fish and spices including turmeric and cumin

vitamin_d_mcg.note: Naturally rich source from fish head

ingredients_notes: Primary ingredients include Red Snapper fish head as the main protein source, coconut milk as the primary fat source, and a rempah spice paste made from dried chillies, shallots, garlic, ginger, galangal, and lemongrass. Tamarind paste provides sourness and Vitamin C. Vegetables include lady's fingers (okra), eggplant, and tomatoes contributing fibre and micronutrients. Fish sauce and salt are significant sodium contributors. Aromatics include curry leaves, fenugreek seeds, and mustard seeds. A curry powder blend of turmeric, coriander, cumin, and chilli is used throughout.
dietary_considerations: High sodium content: 950–1,200 mg per plate, representing approximately 40–50% of the recommended daily intake of 2,000 mg — individuals with hypertension should be mindful
High saturated fat: Coconut milk contributes significantly to saturated fat content, not ideal for those managing cardiovascular risk if consumed frequently
Contains fish allergen: Major allergen present — not suitable for those with fish allergies
May contain shellfish derivatives: Some recipes use shellfish-derived fish sauce — verify with restaurant if shellfish allergy is a concern
Generally gluten-free in authentic preparation: Always verify with the restaurant as thickeners may introduce gluten
Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians: Contains fish and fish sauce
Pescatarian-friendly: Fish-based dish with no land animal meat
High protein: Approximately 38–42g per serving, beneficial for muscle maintenance
Rich in Omega-3 fatty acids: Red Snapper provides beneficial omega-3s supporting heart and brain health
Moderate-to-high caloric density: Primarily driven by coconut milk fat content — opt for less gravy to reduce calorie, sodium, and saturated fat intake
Nutritionally rewarding when consumed occasionally: Provides complete protein, Vitamin B12, Vitamin D, Omega-3 fatty acids, and antioxidant-rich spices such as turmeric (curcumin)
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Singapore preparation methods using approximately 150–180g fish head meat, 150ml coconut milk-based gravy, and mixed vegetables per serving. Values exclude steamed rice. Nutritional values may vary depending on the restaurant, recipe, and exact portion served.

ingredients_notes: Key ingredients include water convolvulus (kangkong), bean sprouts, cucumber, pineapple, green mango, turnip (bangkwang), you tiao (fried dough fritters), fried tofu puffs, and optional cuttlefish. The dish is tossed in a thick sauce made from hae ko (fermented prawn paste), tamarind juice, palm sugar, chilli, and lime, then garnished with crushed toasted peanuts and sesame seeds.
dietary_considerations: Contains shellfish and crustaceans - hae ko (fermented prawn paste) is a core ingredient

High sugar content (~28g per plate) - use caution for diabetic or blood glucose management
Calorie content may vary significantly depending on sauce quantity and ratio of fried to fresh ingredients

ingredients_notes: Thin wheat flour crepe wrapped around braised turnip (jicama/bangkuang) and carrot cooked with dried shrimp, tofu, egg, bean sprouts, and sometimes prawns or pork. Assembled with sweet sauce, chilli sauce, and crushed peanuts and garlic.

ingredients_notes: Thin-sliced white bread (charcoal or toaster grilled), kaya spread (coconut jam made from coconut milk, eggs, sugar and pandan leaves), and thick-cut cold salted butter. Kaya may be Hainanese-style (darker, caramelised) or Nyonya-style (greener, pandan-forward).
dietary_considerations: Contains gluten — not suitable for coeliacs or gluten-intolerant individuals
Contains eggs — not suitable for egg-free or vegan diets
Contains dairy (butter) — not suitable for dairy-free or vegan diets

Suitable for lacto-ovo vegetarians

Refined white bread contributes to fast digestion and quicker return of hunger

ingredients_notes: Yellow egg noodles served in a thick gravy made from sweet potato, beef or chicken stock, dried shrimp, and spices. Garnished with hard-boiled egg, fried tofu, bean sprouts, green chillies, fried shallots, and a squeeze of lime.

Not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to dried shrimp and meat-based stock

Halal-suitable when prepared with chicken or beef stock, as widely available at hawker stalls

zinc_mg.notes: From pork belly and ngoh hiang

vitamin_a_mcg_rae.notes: From egg yolk and dark soy sauce

ingredients_notes: Thick flat yellow noodles in a dark, starchy, gelatinous gravy made from braising liquid, eggs, five-spice powder, and starch thickener. Topped with braised pork belly, ngoh hiang (five-spice meat rolls), fish cake, half a hard-boiled egg, and fried fritters. Served with a vinegar-garlic-chilli condiment. Some versions may include prawns.
dietary_considerations: Very high sodium: 1,400–1,800 mg per bowl, representing 60–75% of the recommended daily intake of 2,000 mg. Not recommended for those with hypertension or kidney conditions.
Contains pork: Not suitable for halal diets unless sourced from a certified halal stall.
Contains gluten: Thick yellow noodles contain wheat flour; soy sauce and ngoh hiang wrappers also contain gluten. Not suitable for coeliac disease or gluten intolerance.
Contains egg: Hard-boiled egg and egg used in gravy thickener are standard components.
Potential shellfish allergen: Some versions include prawns or are prepared in shared environments with shellfish. Verify with the hawker stall.
Moderate to high saturated fat: Braised pork belly contributes significant saturated fat depending on the cut used.
Fried fritters: Inclusion of you tiao (fried dough fritters) adds approximately 50–80 kcal and increases fat content.
Moderate cholesterol: Combination of egg, pork belly, and ngoh hiang contributes meaningfully to dietary cholesterol.
Not suitable for vegetarian or vegan diets.
Typically dairy-free.
Typically nut-free.
Good protein source: Approximately 28–32g of protein per bowl from pork, fish cake, egg, and ngoh hiang.
Suitable for high-protein diets.

diet_suitability.halal: Usually not suitable — contains pork. Check for halal-certified stalls.

data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Singapore hawker-style preparation. diet_suitability.gluten_free: only if rice vermicelli substituted for yellow noodles

ingredients_notes: Rich prawn-pork broth made by slow-simmering prawn heads, shells, and pork ribs. Served with yellow egg noodles and/or rice vermicelli (bee hoon), topped with fresh prawns, sliced pork, pork ribs, hard-boiled egg, bean sprouts, and water kangkong (morning glory). Accompanied by sambal chilli on the side.
dietary_considerations: High sodium content — approximately 1,450–1,650 mg per bowl, representing 65–70% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2,300 mg
Not suitable for halal diets — contains pork ribs and sliced pork
Not suitable for vegetarian or vegan diets — contains prawns, pork, and egg
Not suitable for pescatarian diets — contains pork
Contains major shellfish allergen — prawns
Contains gluten — yellow egg noodles are wheat-based; gluten-free option available by requesting rice vermicelli only
Contains eggs — hard-boiled egg topping
May contain soy — some sambal or seasoning preparations may include soy-based ingredients
Suitable for high-protein diets — approximately 32g protein per bowl from prawns, pork, and egg
Moderate calorie density compared to fried hawker alternatives
Sodium can be reduced by requesting less broth or ordering dry prawn mee
Sambal chilli adds approximately 15–20 kcal per teaspoon and may slightly increase sodium
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Singapore hawker-style preparation. Nutritional values are approximations and may vary depending on the stall, broth concentration, portion size, and specific ingredients used. Percentage daily values are based on a 2,000 kcal adult daily reference intake.

allergens: Tree nuts — candlenuts (buah keras) used in some recipes
Shellfish — belacan (shrimp paste) may be added in some preparations; always verify
ingredients_notes: Key ingredients include slow-braised beef chuck or brisket, full-fat coconut milk, kerisik (toasted desiccated coconut), lemongrass, galangal, ginger, turmeric, dried and fresh chilies, kaffir lime leaves, turmeric leaves, shallots, garlic, tamarind paste, palm sugar, and spices including coriander, cumin, and fennel seeds.
dietary_considerations: High saturated fat (~18g per bowl) due to full-fat coconut milk and kerisik; those monitoring cardiovascular health or cholesterol should be mindful of portion sizes
Moderate sodium at ~680mg per bowl; may vary depending on preparation and added sauces
Calorie-dense relative to volume due to fat content from coconut milk and slow-reduction cooking method
May contain tree nuts (candlenuts) depending on recipe
May contain shellfish allergens if belacan (shrimp paste) is used; always verify with preparer
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Singaporean preparation methods. Values are approximate and may vary depending on the cook, restaurant, or specific recipe variation. Calculated for approximately 250g of beef rendang (meat and sauce, without rice).

ingredients_notes: Jasmine rice cooked with chicken pieces, Chinese sausage (lap cheong), salted fish, dark soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, and optionally mushrooms or vegetables. Served in a traditional clay pot that produces a signature crispy rice crust at the bottom.
dietary_considerations: High sodium content (1,100–1,500 mg per bowl; approximately 48–65% of recommended daily intake of 2,300 mg)
Not suitable for halal diets — contains pork-based Chinese sausage (lap cheong)
Not suitable for vegetarian or vegan diets — contains chicken, salted fish, and oyster sauce
Not gluten-free — soy sauce and oyster sauce contain wheat
Not recommended for low-sodium diets
Contains fish allergen from salted fish
Contains shellfish allergen from oyster sauce
Contains sesame allergen from sesame oil
Contains sulphites from Chinese sausage
Individuals with hypertension or kidney conditions should consume with caution due to high sodium
Requesting less soy sauce or omitting salted fish can reduce sodium by approximately 200–400 mg
Adding leafy greens on the side can improve fiber, Vitamin C, and overall micronutrient profile
Good source of protein (~30g), providing a complete amino acid profile from rice and animal proteins combined
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Singapore hawker-style preparation methods. Values represent a standard single-bowl serving of approximately 420g. Actual nutritional content may vary depending on the specific hawker stall, portion size, and recipe used.

calcium_mg.source_note: From tofu and fish paste items

iron_mg.source_note: From tofu, vegetables, and fish paste

vitamin_c_mg.source_note: From bitter gourd, chili, and vegetables

ingredients_notes: Tofu and various items stuffed with fish paste including bitter gourd, eggplant, chili, and tofu puffs. Served with vegetables, fish balls, and bee hoon (rice vermicelli) in a clear soup broth. May be served with a sweet hoisin-based dipping sauce.
dietary_considerations: High sodium content (900–1,300 mg per bowl; approximately 40–55% of recommended daily intake)
Contains fish and fish paste — not suitable for those with fish allergies
May contain shellfish — some versions include prawn paste
Contains soy — tofu and tofu puffs are soy-based
Contains gluten — soy sauce in broth and hoisin dipping sauce are not suitable for coeliac disease
May contain egg — some fish paste preparations include egg
Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians due to fish paste throughout
Generally suitable for pescatarians
Good low-calorie option relative to other hawker dishes
Good high-protein option
Moderate glycaemic impact — suitable for diabetics if noodle portions are limited
Gluten-free modification possible — request no soy sauce or hoisin sauce and choose rice vermicelli
Low-sodium modification possible — request less broth and avoid dipping sauces
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Singapore hawker-style preparation of Yong Tau Foo with clear soup broth, 6–8 mixed items, and bee hoon (rice vermicelli). Actual values may vary depending on items selected, broth volume, portion size, and individual stall recipes.

iron_mg.notes: From leafy greens (kangkong), meat and seafood

ingredients_notes: Proteins include prawns, chicken, pork, tofu, eggs, or fish. Carbohydrates from white rice, bee hoon (rice vermicelli), or kway teow (flat rice noodles). Vegetables include kangkong, cabbage, bean sprouts, and spring onions. Seasoned with oyster sauce, dark and light soy sauce, fish sauce, sambal chilli, belacan (fermented prawn paste), and sesame oil. Cooked with generous amounts of vegetable oil using high-heat wok frying. Aromatics include garlic, ginger, shallots, and dried chillies.
dietary_considerations: High sodium: 1,200–1,800 mg per serving represents 50–75% of the recommended daily intake of 2,000 mg — caution advised for those with hypertension
Contains shellfish allergens: prawns and belacan (fermented prawn paste)
Contains gluten: soy sauce and oyster sauce
Contains eggs and sesame — not suitable for those with these allergies
May contain pork or lard — not suitable for halal or kosher diets unless specifically prepared otherwise
Generally not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to meat, seafood, oyster sauce, and fish sauce — vegetarian options must be specifically requested
High glycaemic index load from white rice and rice noodles — relevant for diabetics or those managing blood sugar
High caloric density due to generous use of oil in wok cooking
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Singapore Zi Char preparation methods. Values represent a representative single-plate serving of a classic zi char combination and may vary significantly depending on specific dishes ordered, the stall or restaurant, and actual portion sizes. For precise values, consult a registered dietitian or certified food tracking tool.

portion_size: 1 plate (approximately 500-600g including shell, 250-300g edible crab meat with sauce)
ingredients_notes: Primary ingredients include whole Sri Lankan or mud crab (600-800g whole), freshly cracked black pepper, butter, dark soy sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, shallots, curry leaves, light soy sauce, sugar, and cornstarch for sauce thickening.
dietary_considerations: Contains shellfish (crab) — not suitable for those with shellfish allergies
Contains gluten via soy sauce and oyster sauce — not suitable for coeliac disease unless gluten-free alternatives are used
Contains dairy (butter) — not suitable for dairy-free or vegan diets

Moderate to high saturated fat primarily from butter

Exceptional source of zinc and Vitamin B12 supporting immune and neurological health

portion_size: 1 plate (approximately 350-400g, including curry sauce and chicken pieces, served with rice)
ingredients_notes: Bone-in chicken pieces (drumsticks, thighs, wings), full-fat coconut milk, rempah spice paste (shallots, garlic, ginger, galangal, lemongrass, dried chillies), Singapore-style curry powder (coriander, cumin, turmeric, fennel, chilli), curry leaves, pandan leaves, potatoes, fish sauce or light soy sauce, vegetable oil, served with steamed white rice or roti prata
dietary_considerations: High saturated fat due to full-fat coconut milk — individuals monitoring cardiovascular health should be mindful
Moderate to high sodium at 780mg per serving — not recommended for individuals with hypertension without modification
Contains gluten — soy sauce used in preparation; not suitable for celiac disease without substitution
Contains fish — fish sauce used in preparation; not suitable for those with fish allergies
Not vegan or vegetarian — contains chicken and fish sauce
Calorie-dense meal — combination of coconut milk, oil, and rice; best suited for active individuals
Dairy-free — coconut milk used instead of dairy cream
High protein — supports muscle maintenance and repair
Gluten-free option available — substitute tamari for soy sauce
Lower calorie option available — request light coconut milk or smaller rice portion
Lower GI option available — substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice

ingredients_notes: Thin wheat-based dough stretched and folded around a filling of minced mutton or chicken, beaten eggs, onions, garlic, leek or spring onions, cumin, and mixed spices. Pan-fried on a flat griddle with generous amounts of ghee or margarine for a crispy layered exterior. Served with a cucumber and onion pickle in sweet-sour vinegar sauce and optionally a curry dipping sauce.
dietary_considerations: High sodium — approximately 40–48% of recommended daily intake per serving
High cholesterol — approaches general guideline of 300mg per day
High in saturated fat due to ghee used during cooking
Contains gluten — wheat-based dough, not suitable for coeliac disease or gluten intolerance
Contains eggs — not suitable for egg-allergic individuals
Not vegetarian or vegan — contains meat and eggs; vegetarian versions exist but are non-traditional
Typically halal — traditionally prepared with mutton or chicken, suitable for Muslim dietary requirements when sourced from certified halal stalls
Calorie-dense — best enjoyed occasionally as part of a balanced diet
Opting for chicken over mutton can slightly reduce saturated fat content
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Singaporean preparation methods. Nutritional values may vary depending on the hawker stall, portion size, and specific recipe used.

calcium_mg.significance: High — from tofu, especially if calcium-set

ingredients_notes: Deep-fried tofu served on a bed of bean sprouts, cucumber, and sometimes cabbage, topped with a thick sweet-savory peanut sauce made from ground peanuts, shrimp paste (belacan), chillies, and palm sugar (gula melaka). A popular hawker centre dish of Malay-Indonesian culinary heritage.
dietary_considerations: Contains peanuts — major allergen; peanut sauce is a core component
Contains shellfish — belacan (shrimp paste) is typically used; not suitable for shellfish allergies
Contains soy — tofu is soy-based
May contain gluten — some vendors use soy sauce with wheat; verify if gluten-sensitive
Not suitable for vegans in traditional preparation due to shrimp paste
Not suitable for vegetarians in traditional preparation unless belacan is omitted on request
High sodium content (~780–850 mg per plate); not suitable for low-sodium diets
Moderate glycemic impact — peanut sauce contains palm sugar
Deep-frying increases fat content significantly compared to non-fried tofu preparations
Dairy-free
Good plant-based protein source
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Singaporean hawker-style preparation. Nutritional values may vary depending on the vendor, oil absorption during frying, and the quantity of peanut sauce applied. Values are approximated using established food composition databases (USDA, Singapore HPB Food Composition Database).

ingredients_notes: Thin crispy deep-fried pastry shells made from rice flour and plain flour, filled with shredded bangkuang (jicama/yam bean) and julienned carrots braised with dried shrimp (hae bi) and optional crab meat or pork. Topped with sliced hard-boiled egg, fresh prawns, and coriander leaves. Served with sweet chilli sauce and/or sambal belachan.

Contains gluten (wheat flour in pastry shells) - not suitable for coeliac disease or gluten intolerance
Contains egg - relevant for egg allergies
Moderate-to-high sodium content - monitor if on a low-sodium diet
Some versions contain pork - not suitable for halal or kosher diets unless verified
Not suitable for vegetarians or vegans in traditional form due to prawns, dried shrimp, and egg
Pastry shells are deep-fried - moderate consumption advised for heart health

vitamin_c_mg.note: Modest amount from chilli and vinegar-dressed ingredients

allergens.shellfish: possible — some versions include prawn dumplings

ingredients_notes: Thin egg noodles (mee kia) or flat rice noodles (kway teow), seasoned minced pork, briefly blanched pork liver slices, pork balls or dumplings, soy-braised shiitake mushrooms, blanched bean sprouts, sauce base of black vinegar, chilli sauce, soy sauce, sesame oil and lard, crispy lard bits (pork cracklings), garnished with spring onions and fried shallots. Dry-tossed style is the more iconic preparation.

ingredients_notes: Thin rice vermicelli (bee hoon) served in a milky white fish broth made by frying ginger and fish bones before simmering. Topped with fresh sliced fish (typically batang/Spanish mackerel or snakehead fish). Often enriched with evaporated milk. Common accompaniments include lettuce, tomatoes, tofu, and fried shallots.

Contains fish allergen: primary protein source is batang (Spanish mackerel), snakehead, or threadfin fish

Contains dairy allergen: evaporated milk is typically used in the broth

Not suitable for low-carb or keto diets due to rice vermicelli

Suitable for pescatarian diets

Conditionally gluten-free — verify sauces and marinades used with stall

ingredients_notes: Key ingredients include freshly extracted pandan juice or paste for natural green colour and fragrance, coconut milk for richness and tropical sweetness, separated eggs (whipped whites for airy texture), cake or plain flour, caster sugar, vegetable or coconut oil, and baking powder. Commercial versions may use artificial pandan flavouring and green food dye.

Contains eggs — not suitable for egg allergies or vegans
Contains coconut milk — check for tree nut cross-sensitivity

Suitable for lacto-ovo vegetarians

calcium_mg.notes: From attap chee (palm seeds) and red beans

vitamin_c_mg.notes: Trace amounts from grass jelly and syrups

ingredients_notes: Shaved ice with coloured syrups (rose, pandan, sarsi or bandung), sweetened red beans (kacang merah), attap chee (palm seed jelly), grass jelly (cincau), sweet corn kernels, cendol (green pandan rice flour jelly strips), and evaporated or condensed milk drizzled on top. Optional toppings include ice cream, red rubies (water chestnuts in tapioca), and durian paste.
dietary_considerations: High in free sugars — syrups and condensed milk may exceed WHO recommended daily limit of 25g in a single serving
Not recommended for diabetics or those managing blood sugar due to very high glycaemic load
Contains dairy if condensed or evaporated milk is added (standard preparation)
Generally vegetarian-friendly; can be made vegan if dairy milk is omitted or substituted
Typically gluten-free, though cross-contamination at hawker stalls is possible
Low overall allergen risk; attap chee (palm seed) may be unfamiliar to some digestive systems
Low satiety value due to minimal protein and healthy fats despite moderate calorie count
Calorie count can rise to 350–420 kcal if ice cream topping or heavy syrup is added
High water content from shaved ice contributes to hydration
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Singapore hawker-style preparation. Values may vary depending on the stall, syrup quantity, use of condensed milk, and any optional toppings selected.

ingredients_notes: Chendol consists of pandan-flavoured green rice flour jelly strips served over shaved ice, drenched in rich coconut milk and dark palm sugar (gula melaka) syrup. Common additions include red kidney beans. Optional additions at some stalls may include corn, grass jelly, attap chee (palm seeds), or durian, which would alter the nutritional profile.
dietary_considerations: High sugar content: approximately 38–44g per bowl, primarily from gula melaka (palm sugar); high glycaemic dessert unsuitable for frequent consumption by individuals managing diabetes or blood sugar levels
High saturated fat: approximately 10–13g per bowl from coconut milk; those monitoring cardiovascular health should be mindful of portion frequency
Naturally gluten-free: traditional recipe uses rice flour for jelly strips; suitable for gluten-intolerant or coeliac individuals, though vendor verification is advised
Vegan and dairy-free: authentic preparation contains no animal products or dairy; suitable for vegans and lactose-intolerant individuals
Contains legumes (red beans): individuals with legume allergies should exercise caution
Nutritional profile may vary significantly if optional additions such as durian, attap chee, or corn are included
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Singaporean hawker-style preparation. Nutritional values may vary depending on the stall, recipe, and portion generosity.

ingredients_notes: Primary ingredients include glutinous rice flour as the dough base, fresh pandan leaf juice for natural green colouring and flavour, gula melaka (dark palm sugar) as the molten filling, freshly grated coconut as the outer coating, a pinch of salt, and water or coconut milk for dough binding.
dietary_considerations: High in saturated fat due to grated coconut coating — consume in moderation for cardiovascular health
Moderate sugar content from gula melaka — not recommended for diabetics in large quantities
Cholesterol-free and entirely plant-based — suitable for vegans and vegetarians
Made with glutinous rice flour which is gluten-free despite its name — verify cross-contamination risks if commercially purchased
Contains tree nuts/coconut — avoid if allergic to coconut
No shellfish, no dairy, no eggs — safe for those with shellfish, dairy, or egg allergies
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Singaporean preparation methods. Nutritional values may vary depending on piece size, coconut freshness, and quantity of palm sugar used.

ingredients_notes: Key ingredients include rice flour, tapioca starch or sago flour, full-fat coconut milk, white or caster sugar, pandan juice or extract, red or pink food colouring, salt, and water. Contains no eggs, dairy, or meat in its traditional steamed form.
dietary_considerations: Vegan and vegetarian friendly in traditional steamed form
Typically gluten-free as it uses rice flour and tapioca starch; verify with vendor as some recipes may include wheat starch
High sugar content (~18g per slice); individuals managing diabetes or blood sugar should consume in moderation
High saturated fat (~4.9g) derived primarily from full-fat coconut milk; those monitoring cardiovascular health should be mindful of portion size
May contain artificial red or pink food colouring; natural pandan-coloured versions are available
Generally free from major allergens including nuts, shellfish, gluten, dairy, and eggs in traditional form
Cross-contamination possible in commercial kitchen environments
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Singapore preparation methods and standard traditional ingredient ratios. Actual values may vary depending on recipe, ingredient brands, and slice size used by individual vendors or home cooks.

ingredients_notes: Primary ingredients include silken or soft tofu coagulated with gypsum powder (calcium sulfate) or glucono delta-lactone, sweet ginger syrup made from water, sugar, and fresh ginger. Optional additions include pandan leaves in syrup and a soya bean milk base.

Naturally gluten-free in traditional preparation
Contains soy — not suitable for individuals with soy allergies
Moderate to high sugar content (~19g) from added syrup — diabetics should monitor intake
Low sodium — generally suitable for heart-conscious diets
Dairy-free — contains no milk or dairy derivatives

Calcium content is notably high when gypsum is used as coagulant, comparable to a small glass of milk
Reducing syrup quantity can lower sugar and calorie content by up to 30%

ingredients_notes: Fresh pressed sugarcane stalks, crushed or cubed ice. Optional additions include calamansi lime juice, water chestnut, pandan leaf, or lemon. No animal products or major allergens present.

Vegan and plant-based friendly
Gluten-free — safe for coeliac disease or gluten sensitivity
Free from major common allergens including nuts, dairy, shellfish, eggs, soy, and wheat
